quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Solubility product (Ksp)"?

options.text: ΔTf = iKfm; solute particles lower the freezing point by disrupting crystal formation
Concentration in moles of solute per liter of solution (mol/L); the most common concentration unit in chemistry
The equilibrium constant for the dissolution of a sparingly soluble ionic compound; Ksp = product of ion concentrations raised to stoichiometric powers
Increasing pressure (decreasing volume) shifts equilibrium toward the side with fewer moles of gas; inert gas at constant V has no effect

quiz_questions.explanation: "Solubility product (Ksp)" is defined as: The equilibrium constant for the dissolution of a sparingly soluble ionic compound; Ksp = product of ion concentrations raised to stoichiometric powers. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Molar solubility"?

options.text: Gas particles are in constant random motion, collisions are elastic, average KE is proportional to absolute temperature (KE = ³⁄₂kT)
The number of moles of compound that dissolve per liter of saturated solution; calculated from Ksp and stoichiometry
The temperature and pressure at which all three phases coexist in equilibrium (water: 0.01°C, 611.7 Pa)
The minimum energy required for reactants to reach the transition state and form products; determines the rate's temperature sensitivity

quiz_questions.explanation: "Molar solubility" is defined as: The number of moles of compound that dissolve per liter of saturated solution; calculated from Ksp and stoichiometry. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Common-ion effect"?

options.text: A reaction that releases heat to the surroundings; ΔH < 0; products are lower in energy than reactants
-log[H⁺]; a logarithmic scale where each unit represents a 10-fold change in [H⁺]; pH 3 is 1000× more acidic than pH 6
The decrease in solubility of an ionic compound when a solution already contains one of its ions (Le Chatelier's principle applied to dissolution)
Increasing pressure (decreasing volume) shifts equilibrium toward the side with fewer moles of gas; inert gas at constant V has no effect

quiz_questions.explanation: "Common-ion effect" is defined as: The decrease in solubility of an ionic compound when a solution already contains one of its ions (Le Chatelier's principle applied to dissolution). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Selective precipitation"?

options.text: Equilibrium involving species in more than one phase; pure solids and liquids have activity = 1 and are excluded from K
A visual representation showing the relative enthalpies of reactants, products, and intermediates; illustrates exothermic/endothermic steps
Gas particles are in constant random motion, collisions are elastic, average KE is proportional to absolute temperature (KE = ³⁄₂kT)
Using differences in Ksp values to selectively precipitate specific ions from a solution containing multiple ions

quiz_questions.explanation: "Selective precipitation" is defined as: Using differences in Ksp values to selectively precipitate specific ions from a solution containing multiple ions. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Qsp vs. Ksp"?

options.text: The state where forward and reverse reaction rates are equal; concentrations are constant but molecular-level reactions continue
The ONLY disturbance that changes K; increasing T favors the endothermic direction; decreasing T favors the exothermic direction
If Qsp  Ksp: supersaturated, precipitation occurs
Concentration in moles of solute per liter of solution (mol/L); the most common concentration unit in chemistry

quiz_questions.explanation: "Qsp vs. Ksp" is defined as: If Qsp  Ksp: supersaturated, precipitation occurs. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The equilibrium constant for the dissolution of a sparingly soluble ionic compound; Ksp = product of ion concentrations raised to stoichiometric powers"?

options.text: Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution
Pressure/volume change
Solubility product (Ksp)
Chromatography

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Solubility product (Ksp)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The number of moles of compound that dissolve per liter of saturated solution; calculated from Ksp and stoichiometry"?

options.text: Molar solubility
Second law of thermodynamics
Chromatography
Triple point

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Molar solubility".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The decrease in solubility of an ionic compound when a solution already contains one of its ions (Le Chatelier's principle applied to dissolution)"?

options.text: Common-ion effect
Intermediate
Stoichiometric coefficient
Catalyst effect

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Common-ion effect".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Using differences in Ksp values to selectively precipitate specific ions from a solution containing multiple ions"?

options.text: ΔS prediction rules
Nernst equation
Zwitterion
Selective precipitation

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Selective precipitation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "If Qsp  Ksp: supersaturated, precipitation occurs"?

options.text: Qsp vs. Ksp
ΔH from bond energies
Gibbs free energy (ΔG)
Green chemistry

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Qsp vs. Ksp".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Solubility Equilibria is accurate?

options.text: Solubility Equilibria describe the dissolution of sparingly soluble ionic compounds: MₓAᵧ(s) ⇌ xM^(n+)(aq) + yA^(m-)(aq).
Colligative properties (boiling point elevation, freezing point depression, osmotic pressure, vapor pressure lowering) depend on solute particle concentration, not identity.
The half-equivalence point of a weak acid titration is where pH = pKa, useful for buffer preparation.
Stoichiometry is the quantitative study of reactants and products in chemical reactions based on the mole concept.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Solubility Equilibria as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Solubility Equilibria?

options.text: Stoichiometry is the quantitative study of reactants and products in chemical reactions based on the mole concept.
Percent yield (actual/theoretical × 100%) measures reaction efficiency.
The solubility product constant Ksp = [M^(n+)]ˣ[A^(m-)]ʸ.
Zero-order: rate is independent of concentration; first-order: rate doubles when concentration doubles; second-order: rate quadruples when concentration doubles.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Solubility Equilibria as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Solubility Equilibria?

options.text: Atoms consist of protons (p⁺, +1 charge, ~1 amu) and neutrons (n⁰, neutral, ~1 amu) in the nucleus, surrounded by electrons (e⁻, -1 charge, ~1/1836 amu) in orbitals.
Amino acids are polyprotic with both acidic (COOH) and basic (NH₂) groups, existing as zwitterions at physiological pH.
Comparing Qsp to Ksp predicts precipitation: if Qsp > Ksp, a precipitate forms.
The atomic number (Z) equals the number of protons and defines the element.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Solubility Equilibria as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Solubility Equilibria?

options.text: The common-ion effect reduces solubility when a solution already contains one of the ions.
Percent yield (actual/theoretical × 100%) measures reaction efficiency.
The autoionization of water (Kw = [H⁺][OH⁻] = 1.0 × 10⁻¹⁴ at 25°C) connects [H⁺] and [OH⁻].
Breaking bonds requires energy (endothermic), forming bonds releases energy (exothermic).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Solubility Equilibria as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Solubility Equilibria?

options.text: Zero-order: rate is independent of concentration; first-order: rate doubles when concentration doubles; second-order: rate quadruples when concentration doubles.
The Nernst equation adjusts E for non-standard conditions: E = E° - (RT/nF)ln Q.
Selective precipitation exploits different Ksp values to separate ions from solution.
Orbitals are filled in order of increasing energy: 1s, 2s, 2p, 3s, 3p, 4s, 3d, 4p...

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Solubility Equilibria as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Solubility Equilibria?

options.text: Corrosion of iron (rusting): 4Fe + 3O₂ + 6H₂O → 4Fe(OH)₃ → 2Fe₂O₃·3H₂O; prevented by galvanizing (Zn coating) or painting
Nitrogen dioxide equilibrium (2NO₂ ⇌ N₂O₄): the brown-to-colorless color change visually demonstrates equilibrium shifting with temperature changes
Breathalyzer devices using electrochemical oxidation of ethanol to acetic acid, generating a current proportional to blood alcohol concentration
Kidney stone formation: when [Ca²⁺][C₂O₄²⁻] exceeds Ksp of calcium oxalate (2.3 × 10⁻⁹), crystals precipitate in the kidney

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Solubility Equilibria.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Solubility Equilibria?

options.text: Water treatment using selective precipitation: adding Na₂S to remove toxic heavy metals (Pb²⁺, Hg²⁺, Cu²⁺) as insoluble sulfide precipitates (very low Ksp values)

Tempered safety glass as an amorphous solid that, when broken, shatters into small granular pieces rather than sharp shards due to balanced internal stresses from thermal treatment (distinct from laminated glass, which holds together via a...
Supercritical CO₂ used as a green solvent in decaffeination of coffee and dry cleaning, replacing toxic organic solvents
Cold packs using endothermic dissolution of ammonium nitrate (NH₄NO₃) in water to absorb heat and cool injuries

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Solubility Equilibria.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Solubility Equilibria applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Freeze-drying (lyophilization) of food and pharmaceuticals using sublimation under reduced pressure to remove water while preserving structure and nutrients
Solar water heating systems using water's high specific heat capacity to absorb and store thermal energy efficiently
Ocean buffering against acidification: carbonate/bicarbonate system absorbing excess CO₂, but capacity being overwhelmed by current emission rates
Hard water treatment: Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions forming insoluble CaCO₃ scale in pipes and water heaters when Qsp exceeds Ksp upon heating

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Solubility Equilibria.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Solubility Equilibria?

options.text: Carbon dioxide equilibrium in oceans (CO₂ + H₂O ⇌ H₂CO₃ ⇌ HCO₃⁻ + H⁺) affected by rising atmospheric CO₂, causing ocean acidification
Enzyme inhibitors as drugs: statins lower cholesterol by inhibiting HMG-CoA reductase, effectively raising the Ea for cholesterol synthesis
Blood pH maintenance at 7.35-7.45: even small deviations cause acidosis (7.45), both life-threatening conditions
Kidney stone formation: when [Ca²⁺][C₂O₄²⁻] exceeds Ksp of calcium oxalate (2.3 × 10⁻⁹), crystals precipitate in the kidney

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Solubility Equilibria.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Solubility Equilibria in practice?

options.text: Forensic chemistry using stoichiometric analysis to determine the composition of unknown substances from mass spectral data
Kidney dialysis using osmotic pressure principles to remove waste from blood across a semipermeable membrane
Water treatment using selective precipitation: adding Na₂S to remove toxic heavy metals (Pb²⁺, Hg²⁺, Cu²⁺) as insoluble sulfide precipitates (very low Ksp values)
Road salt (NaCl) lowering the freezing point of water to prevent ice formation on roads—effective down to about -9°C (ΔTf = iKfm, i = 2 for NaCl)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Solubility Equilibria.
